content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 24, 1990


Mr. R. Charles Harker, Esq.
American Physical Therapy Association
1111 North Fairfax Street
Alexandria, Virginia 22314

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-691

Dear Mr. Hawker:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

FACTS

The American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) is a
national membership organization with chapters in every state.
All California members of APTA are also members of the California
Chapter-APTA. CAL-PT-PAC is a sponsored committee of the
California Chapter-APTA.

APTA membership dues from California members include a pro-
rata share of dues to the California Chapter-APTA. APTA is
considering adding a line item to the dues invoice to enable
members to earmark an additional amount of funds to CAL-PT-PAC.

In a telephone conversation with me on November 20, 1990, you
confirmed that membership payments are mailed to a bank's lock box
and deposited to APTA's account the day the bank receives the
payment or the next banking day. Later, the bank provides APTA
with an accounting summary of the membership dues received.
Following such notification the appropriate pro-rata share of
California dues are forwarded to the California Chapter-APTA.
APTA would also forward funds earmarked for CAL-PT-PAC.


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, 1990
Page 2

QUESTIONS

1. Is there an advantage for APTA to become a sponsor of
CAL-PT-PAC?

2. Under the circumstances described above, would APTA be
considered an intermediary or an agent and what is the difference
between these designations?

3. What would be the date of receipt for contributions made
to CAL-PT-PAC?

4. What would be the deadline for APTA to report
contributions to CAL-PT-PAC?

5. Will APTA or CAL-PT-PAC be required by the Act to obtain
employer information for contributors to CAL-PT-PAC?

6. If APTA does not learn of the receipt of a contribution
to CAL-PT-PAC on the closing date of the reporting period and
provides the information at a later date, would any liability
arise from the filing by CAL-PT-PAC of an amended campaign
statement?

7. Is it possible for the Commission to provide APTA
examples of how other national associations administer the
collection of membership and political dues for their state
affiliates?

CONCLUSIONS

1 and 2. Because APTA will receive earmarked contributions
for CAL-PT-PAC, APTA will become both a sponsor and an agent of
CAL-PT-PAC. In addition, APTA will act as an intermediary on
behalf of CAL-PT-PAC contributors. Please refer to the analysis
below for a discussion of sponsor, intermediary, and agent
responsibilities.

3. The date of receipt of contributions to CAL-PT-PAC is
the date the membership dues are deposited into APTA's bank
account.

4. APTA must report contributions to CAL-PT-PAC no later
than the closing date of the campaign statement on which the
contributions must be disclosed by CAL-PT-PAC.

Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, 1990
Page 3

5.      APTA must obtain the name, address, occupation and
employer of CAL-PT-PAC contributors.

6.      When an error or omission occurs on a previously filed
campaign statement, CAL-PT-PAC is required to file an amended
statement.   There are no penalties for filing an amended
statement.

7.      For your reference, I have enclosed two advice letters
from associations which have made inquiries similar to those
addressed in this letter. (Leidgh Advice Letter, No. A-89-495 and
Williams Advice Letter, No. A-89-680.)

ANALYSIS

     An organization which receives political contributions
totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for the purpose of
making contributions or expenditures to support or oppose
California state or local candidates or measures is defined in the
Act as a recipient "committee."   (Section 82013(a).)  A recipient
committee is required to file a statement of organization (Form
410) and, among other things, identify all sponsors of the
committee.  (Sections 84101, 84102.)

     Section 82048.7 defines "sponsored committee" as follows:

          (a)  "Sponsored committee" means a committee, other than
     a candidate controlled committee, which has one or more
     sponsors.  Any person, except a candidate or other
     individual, may sponsor a committee.

          (b)  A person sponsors a committee if any of the
     following apply:

               (1)  The committee receives all or nearly all of its
          contributions from the person or its members, officers,
          employees, or shareholders.

               (2)  The person collects contributions for the committee
          by use of payroll deductions or dues from its members,
          officers, or employees.

               (3)  The person, alone or in combination with other
          organizations, provides all or nearly all of the
          administrative services for the committee.

               (4)  The person, alone or in combination with other
          organizations, sets the policies for soliciting contributions
          or making expenditures of committee funds.

                                   (Emphasis added.)

Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, 1990
Page 4


Pursuant to Section 82048.7, because APTA will collect
contributions from its members on behalf of CAL-PT-PAC, APTA will
become a sponsor. Accordingly, within 10 days of APTA becoming a
sponsor, CAL-PT-PAC must amend its statement of organization (Form
410). (Section 84103.)

Since a portion of the members' dues received by APTA will be
"earmarked" for CAL-PT-PAC, APTA will act as an intermediary for
the members' contributions. In addition, because APTA will be
soliciting and receiving contributions on behalf of CAL-PT-PAC,
APTA will be acting as an agent for the committee. (Section
84302; Zweig Advice Letter, No. A-83-228; and Crooks Advice
Letter, No. A-89-464, copies enclosed.) Therefore, APTA will have
responsibilities under the Act as both an intermediary and an
agent, as discussed below.

The 1990 Information Manual C-Campaign Provisions of the
Political Reform Act for General Purpose Recipient Committees
(copy enclosed) states:

     A contribution is "received" the day the candidate
     or committee or his or her agent actually receives
     the contribution, not when the contribution is deposited
     into the committee's bank account.

                              Information Manual C, p. 48
                              (Emphasis added.)

Therefore, CAL-PT-PAC "receives" the contributions on the
date APTA receives the members' dues. Under the facts you have
provided, the date of receipt would be the date a contribution is
credited to APTA's bank account.

With regard to recordkeeping responsibilities, Section 84306
provides:

     ...All contributions received by a person acting as an
     agent of a committee shall be reported promptly to the
     committee's treasurer or any of his or her designated
     agents. "Promptly" as used in this section means not
     later than the closing date of any campaign statement
     the committee or candidate for whom the contribution is
     intended is required to file.

Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, 1990
Page 5


     Accordingly, all contributions received by APTA must be
reported to CAL-PT-PAC no later than the closing date of any
campaign statement the committee is required to file. If APTA
becomes aware of the receipt of a contribution at a later date,
APTA must promptly notify CAL-PT-PAC. Following such
notification, CAL-PT-PAC must file an amended statement disclosing
the contribution.

     Any report or statement filed pursuant to this title
may be amended by the filer at any time. Amending an
incorrect or incomplete report or statement may be
considered as evidence of good faith.

                                        Section 81004.5.

     The Act imposes no penalties for the filing of an amendment.

     Committee treasurers are required to retain the name,
address, occupation and employer of all persons from whom a
contribution of $25 or more is received.2/ (Regulation 18401.)
In addition, the committee must disclose on the campaign statement
the name, address, occupation and employer of each contributor of
$100 or more. Since APTA is both an intermediary and an agent for
contributions to CAL-PT-PAC, APTA must provide to CAL-PT-PAC all
of the information the committee is required to maintain and
disclose. (Sections 84302 and 84211.) However, if APTA and CAL-
PT-PAC have an agreement that CAL-PT-PAC will obtain employer
information for contributors, and CAL-PT-PAC does in fact obtain
and properly disclose the required information, APTA would not
also be required to obtain such information.

     In addition, when a sponsor, acting as an intermediary,
provides its sponsored committee with total contributions of $100
or more, the sponsor must be disclosed on the committee's campaign
statement. (Regulation 18419(c)(3).)

     At your request, I have enclosed advice letters which address
questions similar to those discussed in this letter.



_________________________

2/    At its January 9, 1991 meeting, the Commission will
reconsider whether committees must retain occupation and employer
information for contributors of less $100. You may wish to
contact our office regarding this requirement after that date.

Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, 1990
Page 6


     Please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 if you
have additional questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel
                                        [illegible]
                                        [illegible]
                                        By: Lynda/Cassady
                                            Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures
